title: Basic operators
_authoring: Teaching arc (owner-directed, 2026-06-01): teaching ALWAYS immediately precedes use of the same representation. The OPENING walk (`openingExplanations`) is [AND, then 'Truth tables']: first teach AND (meaning/symbol/code/gate), THEN teach in full what a truth table is and how it works on a concrete grid; the worked example then COMBINES the two (AND written AS a truth table, traced row by row); only THEN does the learner fill in AND's truth table (l1-and). After that, OR is taught JUST-IN-TIME right before the OR item, and NOT right before the NOT item (explanationBeforeNextItem, keyed on KC) — so each operator is taught and then immediately practiced, and the truth-table representation is defined before its first fill-in. Rationale: you cannot ask a learner to fill in a truth table before telling them what one is — and the table must be taught at the moment the lesson first switches into that representation. OR/NOT cards stay in `explanations` (for the just-in-time lookup) but are NOT in the opening walk. Operator cards render their gate + code form (IntroExplanation keyed on `gate`); the truth-table card carries an illustrative AND grid. All truthTables MSB-first. l1-and uses B AND A so the first fill-in forces retrieval, not a copy of the worked example's A & B; l1-review-mix (A AND NOT B) interleaves AND+NOT without nested composition.

lessonIntro.title: Lesson 1 — Logic: AND, OR, NOT
lessonIntro.body:
In logic, every value is one of just two things: TRUE (we write 1) or FALSE (we write 0). Nothing in between. Logic is the set of rules for combining true/false values into new ones — and almost everything in computing is built from three operators that do exactly that: AND, OR, and NOT. We'll start with AND.

By the end of this lesson you'll be able to complete the truth table for AND, OR, and NOT correctly for every input row. You've succeeded when you can fill in any of those three tables from memory, with no hints, without pausing to second-guess which rows are 1.

explanations.body:
AND combines two values and is true ONLY when BOTH of them are true. If either one is false, AND is false.

Think: "it is raining AND it is cold" — true only if both are true.

Symbol:  A & B
In code:  a && b   (true only when a and b are both true)
As a gate: the D-shaped AND gate below — two wires in, one out; the output is on only when both inputs are on.

Next we'll learn how to write down exactly what AND does, for every possible input — using a tool called a truth table.

A truth table is how we write down EXACTLY what an operator does — a small grid that lists every possible input and the result for each. Here is the truth table for AND (the operator you just met).

How to read the grid below:
• The left columns (A, B) are the INPUTS. The right column (Output) is what the operator gives back.
• Each ROW is one possible situation. Read a row across: "if A is this and B is this, the output is that."
• With 2 inputs there are exactly 4 situations, so the table always has 4 rows — A,B = 0,0 then 0,1 then 1,0 then 1,1.

Now combine it with what you know about AND. AND is true only when both inputs are 1, so look down the rows: only the bottom row (A=1, B=1) has output 1; every other row has a 0 somewhere, so its output is 0. Reading the Output column top to bottom — 0, 0, 0, 1 — IS AND, written as a table. In a moment you'll fill one in yourself.

Next operator: OR. It combines two values and is true when AT LEAST ONE of them is true — including when both are. The only way OR is false is when both inputs are false.

Think: "it is raining OR it is cold" — true if either (or both) hold.

Watch out: this is the INCLUSIVE or of logic. Everyday English 'or' often means 'one or the other but not both' — logic's OR says 1 OR 1 is still 1.

Symbol:  A || B
In code:  a || b   (true if a is true, or b is true, or both)
As a gate: the curved OR gate below — the output is on when either input is on.

You already know how to read a truth table — fill in OR's next.

Last operator: NOT. It takes a SINGLE value and flips it: true becomes false, false becomes true. !1 = 0 and !0 = 1. It is the one operator here that takes just one input — so its truth table has only 2 rows.

Think: "it is NOT raining" — true exactly when 'it is raining' is false.

Symbol:  !A
In code:  !a       (true when a is false)
As a gate: the triangle-with-a-bubble below — the little bubble is what means 'invert'. You'll see that same bubble reused on other gates later.

steps.label: List every situation as a row
steps.detail: Two inputs (A, B), each 0 or 1, gives 4 rows: 0,0 / 0,1 / 1,0 / 1,1.

steps.label: Go row by row and ask: is AND true here?
steps.detail: Row A=0,B=0 → not both 1 → output 0. Row 0,1 → not both 1 → 0. Row 1,0 → not both 1 → 0. Row 1,1 → both 1 → output 1.

steps.label: The Output column is the answer
steps.detail: Reading the output top to bottom: 0, 0, 0, 1. Your turn next.
